# Authentication Examples

This directory contains comprehensive examples for all supported authentication methods in the Databricks SDK for JavaScript.

## Quick Reference

| Example                                                          | Use Case                          | Environment   | Credentials Needed              |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ------------- | ------------------------------- |
| [oauth-m2m.ts](./oauth-m2m.ts)                                   | CI/CD, automation                 | Any           | Client ID, client secret        |
| [oauth-u2m.ts](./oauth-u2m.ts)                                   | Interactive apps with SSO         | Local/Desktop | Client ID, browser              |
| [config-file-default.ts](./config-file-default.ts)               | Local development                 | Local         | ~/.databrickscfg                |
| [config-file-profile.ts](./config-file-profile.ts)               | Multi-workspace management        | Local         | ~/.databrickscfg with profiles  |
| [azure-cli.ts](./azure-cli.ts)                                   | Local development                 | Azure         | Azure CLI (`az login`)          |
| [azure-msi.ts](./azure-msi.ts)                                   | Azure VMs, App Service, Functions | Azure         | Managed Identity                |
| [azure-client-secret.ts](./azure-client-secret.ts)               | Azure automation                  | Azure         | Service principal credentials   |
| [google-default-credentials.ts](./google-default-credentials.ts) | GCP services, local development   | GCP           | Default credentials or `gcloud` |
| [google-service-account.ts](./google-service-account.ts)         | GCP automation                    | GCP           | Service account key             |
| [aws-instance-profile.ts](./aws-instance-profile.ts)             | EC2 instances                     | AWS           | IAM instance profile            |
| [environment-detection.ts](./environment-detection.ts)           | Flexible apps (recommended)       | Any           | Any supported method            |
| [custom-credentials.ts](./custom-credentials.ts)                 | Advanced custom integration       | Any           | Custom secret management        |

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

All examples require:

- Node.js 18.0 or higher
- TypeScript 4.5 or higher (or ts-node for running examples)
- Databricks SDK installed: `npm install @databricks/sdk-experimental`

### Running Examples

Each example can be run directly (they have a shebang):

```bash
./examples/auth/<example-name>.ts
```

Or with npx ts-node:

```bash
npx ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register examples/auth/<example-name>.ts
```

## Authentication Methods Overview

### 1. Personal Access Token (PAT)

Legacy authentication method. Prefer other methods that don't require long-lived tokens.

**Best for:** Development, testing, simple scripts

**Examples:**

- [pat-env-var.ts](./pat-env-var.ts) - Credentials from environment variables ✅

**Recommended**

**Setup:**

1. Go to Databricks workspace → User Settings → Access Tokens
2. Click "Generate New Token"
3. Copy the token value
4. Set environment variables:

```bash
export DATABRICKS_HOST="https://your-workspace.cloud.databricks.com"
export DATABRICKS_TOKEN="dapi..."
```

### 2. OAuth Machine-to-Machine (M2M)

**Best for:** Production services, CI/CD pipelines, automation

**Example:** [oauth-m2m.ts](./oauth-m2m.ts) ✅ **Recommended for production**

**Setup:**

1. Create service principal in Databricks account console
2. Generate OAuth client ID and secret
3. Grant service principal permissions to workspace
4. Set environment variables:

```bash
export DATABRICKS_HOST="https://your-workspace.cloud.databricks.com"
export DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id"
export DATABRICKS_CLIENT_SECRET="your-client-secret"
```

### 3. OAuth User-to-Machine (U2M)

**Best for:** Interactive desktop applications, tools with SSO

**Example:** [oauth-u2m.ts](./oauth-u2m.ts)

**Setup:**

1. Register OAuth app in Databricks account console
2. Configure redirect URI (typically `http://localhost:8020`)
3. Copy the client ID
4. Set environment variables:

```bash
export DATABRICKS_HOST="https://your-workspace.cloud.databricks.com"
export DATABRICKS_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id"
```

### 4. Configuration File

**Best for:** Local development, managing multiple workspaces

**Examples:**

- [config-file-default.ts](./config-file-default.ts) - Use DEFAULT profile
- [config-file-profile.ts](./config-file-profile.ts) - Use named profiles

**Setup:**

Create `~/.databrickscfg`:

```ini
[DEFAULT]
host = https://your-workspace.cloud.databricks.com
token = dapi...

[PROD]
host = https://prod.cloud.databricks.com
token = dapi...

[STAGING]
host = https://staging.cloud.databricks.com
token = dapi...
```

### 5. Azure Authentication

**Best for:** Applications running on Azure or using Azure credentials

**Examples:**

- [azure-cli.ts](./azure-cli.ts) - Local development with `az login`
- [azure-msi.ts](./azure-msi.ts) - Azure Managed Identity (no secrets!)
- [azure-client-secret.ts](./azure-client-secret.ts) - Azure Service Principal

**Azure CLI Setup:**

```bash
az login
az account set --subscription <subscription-id>
export DATABRICKS_HOST="https://adb-<workspace-id>.<random>.azuredatabricks.net"
```

**Azure MSI Setup:**

1. Enable managed identity on Azure resource (VM, App Service, etc.)
2. Grant managed identity permissions to Databricks workspace
3. Deploy your application - no credentials needed!

### 6. Google Cloud Platform Authentication

**Best for:** Applications running on GCP or using GCP credentials

**Examples:**

- [google-default-credentials.ts](./google-default-credentials.ts) - Default credentials
- [google-service-account.ts](./google-service-account.ts) - Service account

**GCP Setup:**

```bash
gcloud auth application-default login
export DATABRICKS_HOST="https://your-workspace.gcp.databricks.com"
```

### 7. AWS Authentication

**Best for:** Applications running on AWS EC2

**Example:** [aws-instance-profile.ts](./aws-instance-profile.ts)

**AWS Setup:**

1. Create IAM role with Databricks permissions
2. Attach role to EC2 instance as instance profile
3. Deploy your application - credentials auto-detected!

### 8. Environment Detection

**Best for:** Applications that need to work across multiple environments

**Example:** [environment-detection.ts](./environment-detection.ts) ✅ **Recommended for flexibility**

The SDK automatically tries authentication methods in this order:

1. Explicit configuration
2. Environment variables
3. Configuration file
4. Cloud provider authentication

### 9. Custom Credentials

**Best for:** Advanced use cases with custom secret management

**Example:** [custom-credentials.ts](./custom-credentials.ts)

Use cases:

- Fetch from AWS Secrets Manager / Azure Key Vault / GCP Secret Manager
- Custom token refresh logic
- Corporate authentication systems
- Dynamic credential rotation

## Choosing the Right Authentication Method

### For Development

✅ **Recommended:** [config-file-default.ts](./config-file-default.ts)

### For Production

✅ **Recommended:** [oauth-m2m.ts](./oauth-m2m.ts) (Service Principal)

### For CI/CD

✅ **Recommended:** [oauth-m2m.ts](./oauth-m2m.ts) with secrets management

### For Cloud Services

- **Azure:** [azure-msi.ts](./azure-msi.ts) ✅ **Best - no secrets!**
- **GCP:** [google-default-credentials.ts](./google-default-credentials.ts)
- **AWS:** [aws-instance-profile.ts](./aws-instance-profile.ts) ✅ **Best - no secrets!**

### For Interactive Applications

✅ **Recommended:** [oauth-u2m.ts](./oauth-u2m.ts) (SSO support)

### For Multi-Workspace Management

✅ **Recommended:** [config-file-profile.ts](./config-file-profile.ts)

## Security Best Practices

### ✅ Do

- Use environment variables for credentials in production
- Use OAuth M2M (service principals) for automation
- Use cloud-native authentication (MSI, instance profiles) when available
- Rotate credentials regularly
- Use configuration profiles for development
- Store secrets in secure secret managers

### ❌ Don't

- Hard-code credentials in source code
- Commit credentials to version control
- Share PAT tokens between users or applications
- Use long-lived credentials when short-lived are available
- Store credentials in plain text files in production

## Troubleshooting

### Authentication Failed

1. **Check credentials:** Verify host URL and credentials are correct
2. **Check permissions:** Ensure user/service principal has workspace access
3. **Check network:** Verify connectivity to Databricks workspace
4. **Check environment:** Ensure environment variables are set correctly

### Common Issues

**Issue:** `Cannot find module '@databricks/sdk-experimental'`

**Solution:** Install the SDK: `npm install @databricks/sdk-experimental`

**Issue:** `Authentication failed: 401 Unauthorized`

**Solution:** Check that your token/credentials are valid and not expired

**Issue:** `Authentication failed: 403 Forbidden`

**Solution:** Check that your user/service principal has permissions to the workspace

**Issue:** Azure CLI authentication fails

**Solution:** Run `az login` and `az account set --subscription <id>`

**Issue:** GCP authentication fails

**Solution:** Run `gcloud auth application-default login`

## Testing Your Authentication

All examples include a simple test that:

1. Creates a client with your chosen authentication method
2. Calls `currentUser.me()` to verify authentication
3. Prints user information on success
4. Shows error details on failure

This provides immediate feedback on whether your authentication is working correctly.
